chapters.title: Cultivate awareness within and a round
chapters.text:
Most of us have a somewhat absurd perception of philosophy. It frequently seems like an intimidating venture that sucks the fun out of life and is only adopted by individuals looking for answers to complicated questions.

But Stoicism is nothing like that — it’s not a form of philosophy that will make your life miserable. It is a tool for making your existence fulfilling. And if you want to live your life in line with the disciplines and virtues of Stoicism, you will need to have a deep self-awareness and a clear perception of the world around you.

Like any other branch of philosophy, the first essential requirement for Stoicism is clear thinking which stems from self-examination. Among the legendary proponents of Stoicism was Epictetus. He stated that we become philosophers when we exercise our guiding reason and question our beliefs, emotions, and language.

An important place to begin in philosophy is this: a clear perception of one’s own ruling principle. ~ Epictetus

Stoicism cultivates inner strength, resilience, and acceptance in the face of life's challenges, rather than being ruled by external circumstances.

Underestimating ourselves is detrimental. We must appreciate each achievement we make and every brave act we manage to execute. We should face both our weaknesses and our strengths: they are essential. They make us human. By making a clean assessment of ourselves, we’ll manage to look inward and discover what exactly we are capable of.

Stay tuned to discover what it means to be Stoic and the benefits of practicing Stoic philosophy.

chapters.title: Sharpen focus by clarifying thoughts
chapters.text:
Every day may spiral into a suffocating cycle of overthinking. Do I look good? Should I go there? Am I reaching my potential? Have I made any progress? What comes next? Am I good at anything?

Indeed, with all the distractions around, modern life is overwhelming. But amid all these distractions, it’s possible to stay focused.

Control your perceptions. Direct your actions properly. Willingly accept what’s outside your control. ~ Ryan Holiday & Stephen Hanselman

We should follow the lead of NFL coach Bill Belichick, who simply tells the players, “Do your job.” Who we are doesn’t matter — artists, coders, baristas, or managers. All we have to do is focus on the task and do it to the best of our abilities. It’s not our job to get lost in myriads of thoughts and other people’s business.

Living in the moment is essential because it allows us to fully appreciate the present and make the most of our experiences. When mindful and present, we can engage more fully with our surroundings and enjoy the beauty and wonder of the world around us. It can also help to reduce stress and anxiety, as we are not worrying about the future or ruminating on the past. Additionally, being present can improve our relationships with others, as we can be fully engaged and attentive in conversations and interactions.

Marcus Aurelius, the Roman Emperor, advised that we should always approach each task as if it’s the last thing we’ll ever do on Earth. This way, we will manage to focus on what matters and not give in to distractions.

Marcus Aurelius also advised using a mantra to keep our minds focused. As soon as the world starts getting us down, we need a reminder to stay determined: “I know the truth, and I am capable of doing my job.” We need to repeat the mantra to keep our minds clear and focused.

You can also keep focus and fix your attention on what is essential by accepting that you can’t control everything. Some things are just beyond human powers.

Realizing your limits can drastically reduce the number of things you need to worry about.

chapters.title: Taming emotions for wiser actions
chapters.text:
Theodore Roosevelt is a person of interest with a stoic point of view. He was a powerful man but also had an addictive personality with compulsive tendencies. Roosevelt couldn’t live without work and physical activities. He had a near-death experience while exploring a river in the Amazon and needlessly killed thousands of animals while traveling in Africa. Roosevelt also begged to join the armed forces during World War I, though he was almost 69.

Like Roosevelt, many of us are driven by something beyond our control. Calmness scares us, and we search for some kind of destruction. We choose disharmony when peace is, in fact, the more reputable and fitting choice. To avoid this mistake, always let intelligence and reason take the lead whenever you make a crucial decision. Put your emotions in check and stop giving in to them impulsively.

Controlling emotions is easier said than done — how do we do this? The answer lies in mindfulness and practice. As soon as you feel a wave of intense feelings rushing in, take a moment to breathe. If the emotions are too fierce, notice how they manifest in your body. Is your heart beating too fast? Do your hands feel blazing hot or, on the contrary, cold as ice? Try to relax your body gradually — rub your hands or hug yourself. Focus on your breathing.

Start the analysis when you feel better or the emotions aren’t intense. Talk with someone about your feelings or write them down if possible. This assessment will make your emotions more structured and understandable.

Journaling is an excellent mindfulness practice. It can help reduce symptoms of anxiety and depression, improve sleep quality, and boost self-esteem. It can also serve as a form of self-care and a way to track personal growth.

Being attuned to your feelings will provide you with a sense of stability and clarity.

Naturally, we humans are wired to give in to our emotions. However, as a Stoic, you must learn to control your emotions and avoid letting them cloud your judgment.

chapters.title: Be aware of cognitive biases to retain clarity of reasoning
chapters.text:
As humans, we often see ourselves as intelligent and wise, but the truth is, everything in the world has its limits, and there are things our minds can’t even comprehend. The Stoics are aware of this fact, and it’s clear that the key to improving ourselves and living a better life is to be mindful of our biases and stay humble.

The human mind is a beautiful creature: it can form a belief in a second. We frequently form split-second opinions. Sometimes they are based on years of knowledge and experience. Other times, they might be entirely irrational. That’s where prejudice and stereotypes come onto the stage. This tendency is a flaw of the human condition.

Taking a moment to analyze our thoughts makes us more mindful.

Ask yourself questions like, “Just how bad is this situation?” or “Why are my feelings so intense?” This inner conversation will test your instincts and split-second assumptions, making you rethink your decisions. Increased mindfulness helps you fight biased opinions and act on impulse.

Living as a Stoic can bring you great enjoyment. But to get there, you need to notice and eliminate destructive habits that might be ingrained in your mind. Awareness of harmful thoughts is the first step to creating a positive mindset.

Marcus Aurelius was an ardent proponent of mindfulness, and he encouraged his students to observe their thoughts and origins. He advised asking the following questions to increase self-awareness:

1. Where does this thought come from?
2. Are they biased?
3. Is their nature constructive or destructive?
4. Are they pushing me to do something I’ll regret later?

Identify patterns your brain operates on and understand what compels you to act. Mindfulness is one of the essential steps on the road to creating a happier and more fulfilling life. It’ll increase your self-awareness and show you which habits you must abandon and what practices you should cultivate. You’ll break free from destructive patterns, replace them with better ones and find more enjoyment in your life. This way, you’ll achieve something great — inner peace.

chapters.title: Obstacles are a part of life
chapters.text:
While planning is excellent, things don’t always go as expected. Things happen, people get in the way, and situations go wrong from time to time. True success comes from finding different ways around obstacles.

The Stoic mind views things from the same perspective. Stoics aren’t entirely preoccupied with preparations and don’t have a plan for every tiny detail in their lives. But they don’t fret: no matter what happens, they are confident that adaptation will fix everything.

Stoics have a flexible outlook on life. They don’t search for instructions. They focus on independence and creativity. Stoics pay great attention to cultivating confidence and finding unexpected solutions to problems. This way, they are resilient and mentally prepared to see the opportunity in every challenge they face instead of being rigid.

We can adopt Stoic practices and challenge any obstacles considering that we always have a backup option. Any plan can face an unexpected change, and so can our minds. It can switch between perceptions and create solutions to most problems.

Every obstacle opens a way for new opportunities. If a friend hurts us, we can use this situation as a learning moment. We can either learn to establish our boundaries and cease communication with that person. Or, if we value friendship to a high degree, we can develop forgiveness. If our work disappears from our laptop due to a technical issue, we can use this as an opportunity to train your communication skills. We’ll have to explain to your boss where our work went and whatnot. You can also use this opportunity to create a new, better version of the work that disappeared.

Also, apart from having a flexible outlook on life, Stoics know when to stick and when to quit. If they notice something is not working, they just let it go instead of going to extremes to make it work.

Your decisions don’t have to be final: you can always change your mind if you find a more exciting direction.

Of course, having a flexible perspective is not an excuse for being lazy or irresponsible. It takes courage to decide to make a change and do things differently. It also takes discipline and awareness to know that the sentiment of “Oh, but this looks even better” is a temptation that shouldn’t be indulged endlessly.

chapters.title: Embrace fate and let it motivate you
chapters.text:
Imagine a situation when something unpleasant happened to you. You can change one thing: the past event or your perception of it. Which would be easier? The answer is clear. You hold the power to accept the things that happened and abandon regret. In Stoicism, this practice is called the “art of acquiescence.” Acceptance replaces fighting things that don’t matter.

Experienced stoics take it one step further. They don’t only accept the event; they also find joy in them — no matter what. Amor fati, they say. It translates as the love of fate. Amor fati is a philosophical concept that encourages individuals to embrace and accept their fate rather than resent it. Adopting a positive outlook toward one's circumstances can cultivate greater resilience and help one find meaning and purpose in life. This skill goes beyond acceptance: experienced stoics enjoy every event.

Stoics adapt their desires to circumstances rather than pointlessly wishing they could change events and make things turn out the way they want.

None of us have any say in the outcome of all our ventures. It doesn’t matter how much effort you put into an endeavor. Fate and luck have their way of influencing your life. The only thing you can do is embrace it.

Believing in a higher power, in the real sense, is about attuning to the universe and ridding ourselves of the toxic mindset that we’re at the center of.

It’s not your obligation to think that some higher power controls everything in the universe. But you must abandon believing you can oversee everything. Changing this thinking pattern will simplify your life and make you happier. You’ll quit the most potent addiction: control.

However, we must remember that accepting fate is not to be confused with passivity or seen as an excuse to live apathetically.

Did you know? When he was 39, Franklin D. Roosevelt discovered he had an illness: polio. Though he endured many struggles and could’ve succumbed to the victim mentality, he decided to stay strong and pursue his ambitions. The rest is history: FDR went on to be elected to the presidency four times.

chapters.title: Embrace mortality's power without distress
chapters.text:
In life, everything and everyone must eventually come to an end. As disciples of Stoicism, we must accept that death is the end of the journey for us all rather than despise it or tremble at the thought of it.

Of course, we don’t have to live our lives thinking and fretting about death, but we should never forget that it’s inevitable.

Death is our constant companion, lurking in the corner and waiting to strike. This threat may influence us in two ways: we may spend our lives constantly fearing it or turn dread into motivation and aspire to fulfill our time on earth.

It’s easy to get stuck in the petrifying cycle of running from death. If we choose this road, fear will constantly cloud our judgment, keeping us away from enjoying our lives to the fullest. Realizing and accepting our mortality is one of our most important decisions.

What are the experiences that make us realize the inevitability of death? It may take something drastic and unexpected, such as a car accident, to recognize that our time is limited. The realization may also come from unjust and unfair events, such as living during a full-scale war. Watching your people get their lives taken from them every day is a painful reminder about mortality. What we can do when we experience such events is take this horror and use our emotions as fuel to keep fighting. We may use this mantra as our guide, “When people die every day to keep me safe, the least I can do is abandon fear and do my job.”

There’s no resource more valuable than time. Our main objective should be to use it to its full potential. So, use today — every day — to do good as often as possible and be satisfied with what you have been given.

The world is undoubtedly an unequal place, but death? That’s something you can’t run from. It’s something that comes to everyone.

Death is an equalizer: no matter who you are or where you come from, you can’t escape it.

When your confidence jumps to the skies of places or falls to the deepest holes, remember your final destination. Death doesn’t discriminate between the sinners and the saints — it takes, and it takes.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Stoicism is a tool for living a good life. It provides a set of guiding principles that can help you make better choices. Ancient Stoics believed the world’s order was a natural phenomenon; therefore, every aspect of life is interconnected to fulfill a specific duty. As a Stoic disciple, your primary mission is to seek virtuousness. Stoics placed so much value on virtue because it’s the highest purpose one can commit to. A life lived in virtuousness is a life lived in honesty, discipline, and courage.

We tend to seek things that have artificial meaning. Money frequently becomes the center of our attention, as if it holds all answers to success and fulfillment. We face the harsh reality only when we achieve our materialistic dreams: money doesn’t bring happiness. They provide stability and a platform for achieving other goals, but they should never be our focus point.

Virtue, however, is nothing like that : it holds immense value. Our actions determine whether we’re virtuous people. Of course, the price you pay for virtuousness is to strive for it voluntarily — no one will force you to do the right thing. As you continue your journey in Stoicism by implementing all its guiding principles, you will come to believe in your abilities. Furthermore, through perseverance and staunch resolution, you will improve yourself and positively impact the lives of those around you.

Try this


• Never overestimate your abilities, but don’t undersell yourself, either.
• Establish a good connection with your emotions. Apply the mindfulness approach to get a better understanding of those emotions.
• Measure your actions and the reasons behind them. Try to figure out the direction they are leading you toward. If the path doesn’t make you happy, stop.

Read it till the end?
